body_txt: A subtrope of Technology Marches On that usually takes the form of a young character asking an adult one what a certain piece of physical media is, initially fueled by the novelty of the notion that a format of media could actually become obsolete. It bears mentioning that it started cropping up in fiction before 1990, and the characters unfamiliar with records were full-grown adults, meaning that in its earliest form the trope ran on shameless hyperbole. Even in the 21st century its realism is dubious . Most young people do know what a vinyl record is due to Pop Culture Osmosis , even if they never listened to or owned one themselves and don't know whether a "seven inch" was an album or a single, or whether 45 rpm came before or after 33 1/3. Modern DJs still use them, for instance, and they've also been undergoing a resurgence of popularity among niche music fans thanks to their retro appeal and their exemption from the abuses of the Loudness War , and even outsold CDs for the first time in over 30 years in 2019. Similar cases apply to VCR , music cassettes , and Plug 'n' Play Games . This trope is far more justified with media formats that did not leave a huge pop culture imprint and would be obscure to anyone outside the relevant decade. 8-Track, Betamax, Laserdisc, and MiniDisc were all touted as the Next Big Thing but were overshadowed by a competing format. Someone who was around in the 80's might recognize a Laserdisc, but anyone younger will almost certainly be perplexed by what they would perceive as a record-sized DVD. Granted, whether or not a young person knows what the item is doesn't mean they have the means to play it. If the item is a plot-relevant MacGuffin , finding the appropriate device for it can be an ordeal in and of itself. Basically, what Before My Time is to cultural references, this trope is to technology. Not to be confused with the record label of the same name, owned by David Wilcox . Contrast Technologically Blind Elders , the inverse. See also Popularity Polynomial , when a trend is popular in one generation, becomes neglected, and later is rediscovered as nostalgic by the next generation.
